Small aged tensile samples showed that aging reduced the fracture strain at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
25 and 188 formed carbides and Laves phase.
Hastelloy N is a solution-strengthened nickel-base alloy that was developed for good strength and corrosion resistance at about $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
No intermetallic compounds have been identified in this alloy, but carbides precipitate and cause modest changes in the properties. $^{1,2}$ Haynes alloys Nos.
25 and 188 are cobalt-base alloys that are solid solution strengthened and suitable for use at temperatures up to $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

These cobalt-base alloys experience some property changes due to carbide precipitation, but the formation of brittle Laves phases of the $\mathrm{A}_2\mathrm{B}$ type is the main cause of embrittlement. $^{3-5}$
188 up to 4000 hr at temperatures from 650 to $900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and measuring their impact properties at 25 and $300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Standard Charpy V-notch impact specimens were machined in conformance with the specifications in ASTM Standard E 23-66.
The samples were solution annealed $1\mathrm{hr}$ at $1150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in argon and cooled rapidly to ambient temperature.
They were wrapped in nickel foil and sealed in quartz in argon at 0.33 atm and aged at various temperatures.
Following aging they were broken from the capsule and impact tested at 25 and $300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ according to ASTM Standard E 23-66.
The results of impact tests on Hastelloy N at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ are shown in Fig.
The material was quite tough in the solution-annealed condition, with an impact energy of 164 ft-lb.
Aging at 650 and $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ caused a gradual decrease in the impact energy to values of 75 and 95 ft-lb, respectively, and then the impact energy increased with further aging.
Aging at $900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ resulted in a steady decrease to about 30 ft-lb after aging 4000 hr.
Aging at $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ had little effect for 500 hr, but longer aging resulted in a rapid decrease in the impact energy to a value of 40 ft-lb after aging 4000 hr.

The impact energies were higher at $300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , with a value of 235 ft-1b for the solution-annealed material and a minimum value of 70 ft-1b for samples aged at $900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
All the samples had fibrous fractures, and three of the samples tested at $300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ had sufficient toughness not to part.

Small tensile specimens having a gage section 1/2 in. long and 1/8 in. in diameter were machined from halves of several tested impact specimens.
These specimens were tensile tested at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ at a strain rate of 0.1/min, and the results are summarized in Table 2.
Although aging Hastelloy N at $900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for 100 hr reduced the impact energy from 164 to 82 ft-lb (Fig.
4) the uniaxial tensile properties changed very little (Table 2, specimens 11440 and 11441).

Aging for 4000 hr at $900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ reduced the impact energy to 35 ft-lb, and the tensile properties indicate a general decrease in yield and tensile strength, very small changes in axial strain, and a sizeable decrease in the reduction in area (Table 2, specimens 11440 and 11448).

Carbides of the $\mathbf{M}_2\mathbf{C}$ and $\mathbf{M}_6\mathbf{C}$ types are formed in Hastelloy N, but the $\mathbf{M}_2\mathbf{C}$ type should predominate in a heat such as 6960, which contained only $0.05\%$ Si.
The "M" in this carbide consists of about $80\%$ Mo and $20\%$ Cr.
Aging at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ caused a decrease in the impact energy for the first 500 hr and a slight increase with further aging (Fig.

The microstructural change observed at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ was carbide precipitation along the grain boundaries, and no obvious characteristic of this precipitation could account for the slight minimum in the impact energy.

Wlodek<sup>3</sup> has studied the embrittlement of Haynes alloy No.
25 and the phases that are formed.
He found that the solution-annealed alloy contained $\mathrm{M}_{6}\mathrm{C}$ and $\mathrm{Zr}(\mathrm{N},\mathrm{C})$ .
Aging over the range 650 to $1100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ formed $\mathrm{Cr}_{23}\mathrm{C}_{6}$ and a Laves phase, $\mathrm{A}_{2}\mathrm{B}$ .
After prolonged aging the major constituents were $\mathrm{M}_{6}\mathrm{C}$ and the Laves phase.
15; it contains a primary carbide, likely $\mathrm{M}_{6}\mathrm{C}$ .

5), the microstructure changed only slightly.
After aging for 4843 hr at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (Fig.
16) the grain boundaries etched very quickly (likely because of the presence of carbide or Laves precipitate), and some precipitate was visible in the grains.
Aging at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ produced a steady decrease in the impact energy (Fig.
5), and large amounts of precipitate (likely Laves phase) were visible in the microstructure (Fig.

5), but the microstructure showed little change from the solution-annealed condition (compare Figs.

Herchenroeder<sup>4</sup> studied the aging characteristics of four heats of Haynes alloy No.

He found that the solution-annealed alloy contained primary $\mathbf{M}_6\mathbf{C}$ and that aging formed secondary $\mathbf{M}_6\mathbf{C}$ , $\mathbf{M}_{23}\mathbf{C}_6$ , and a Laves phase of the $\mathbf{A}_2\mathbf{B}$ type.
Over the range 700 to $900^{\circ}\mathbf{C}$ the $\mathbf{M}_6\mathbf{C}$ tended strongly to transform to $\mathbf{M}_{23}\mathbf{C}_6$ on aging.

The fractures of the nine tensile samples described in Table 2 (p.
10) were examined in the scanning electron microscope.
26 of the Haynes alloy No.
25 tensile specimens show that the fractures are primarily intergranular.
All three alloys underwent substantial losses in toughness as a result of aging over the temperature range 650 to $900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Hastelloy N forms only carbides during aging, but the alloy lost about $80\%$ of its toughness during aging at 800 and $900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (Fig.
Aging at 650 and $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ caused losses of about $50\%$ .
